Students learn close, critical reading and analysis and compose argument, rhetorical analysis, and synthesis essays; readings focus on both nonfiction and fiction texts. Typically, passing the AP Language and Composition exam earns three (3) credits for freshman composition (e.g., English 1010). Some colleges offer both freshman and sophomore composition, six credits (6), for passing with a high score. Check with the colleges you plan to attend for their AP credit awards policies.

name: English 12: Music As Literature
description: This is an English 12 or Fine Arts elective course, and students may choose which .50 credit they would like to earn at registration. Music Language Arts explores the power of rhetoric combined with music theory in popular culture throughout history—from Catholic composers working surreptitiously in the courts of Anglican kings to the record-breaking artistry of Taylor Swift; from heartbroken cowboys to Vietnam protests—Civil War songs to African American Spirituals. Sung music provides a detailed record of culture, values, and zeitgeist, which we will analyze for its artistic merit, historical significance, and use of persuasive, argumentative, and expressive language. We will study the music elements of rhythm, harmonic progression, meter, and melodic composition while exploring the rhetorical situation, rhetorical devices, figurative language, and rhetorical appeals as the universal vehicles for carrying language across geographic borders, cultures, peoples, and time.

name: English 12: Humanities
description: Humanities studies the fundamentally human need to share our experience through creative artistic expression--to be citizens of a community beyond ourselves. We'll understand and enjoy various art forms (visual arts, performing arts, architecture, and literature) by thinking critically about their formal compositional elements, historical context, and principles of creative production. We’ll also apply these creative principles to how we create everyday, from athletics to everyday fashion and design. We’ll become creators ourselves. Perhaps most importantly, we'll understand that collaboratively sharing creative expression is a powerful way to improve our own character by increasing empathy for and connection to others.

name: AP English Lit
description: AP Literature is an introductory, college level literature course focused on works of fiction, drama, and poetry from various periods and cultures. Students learn close, critical reading and analysis and compose interpretive essays. Typically, passing the AP Literature exam earns three (3) credits general education (GE) credit humanities distribution/Introduction to Literature credit. Some colleges offer this credit for both the freshman and sophomore levels, six credits (6), for passing with a high score. Check with the colleges you plan to attend for their AP credit awards policies. COLLEGE WRIT (College Writing 1010*) ENCW1/2 (ALA) *UVU credit may be earned Course Description: College Writing Concurrent Enrollment follows Utah Valley University’s (UVU) required curriculum for freshman composition and awards college credit for English 1010 upon successful completion of the course. Students will read assigned textbook chapters from UVU’s assigned text, complete chapter submissions, draft and complete multiple revisions of essays in different genres, and more. The course is both reading and writing intensive. The course instructor utilizes UVU’s CANVAS course for assignment submissions, virtual discussions, and access to lesson materials and schedule. Though a hybrid-online course, as it is simultaneously a high school course, attendance is required. (Please take a fully online, asynchronous course if in-person attendance is an issue.) Students must register with and become UVU students in order to earn the credit and to schedule mandatory conferences with UVU’s Writing Center tutors. The difference between College Writing Concurrent Enrollment at THS and English 1010 at UVU is that at UVU the course is a single semester; at THS the course is year-long (i.e. two semesters), and sufficient grades also earn four ELA quarter credits towards high school graduation as well as English 1010 credit through UVU.

name: Art History 1010
description: This is a UVU Concurrent Enrollment course that will give an Art 1010 credit. In this class, we will learn about cultures around the world based on themes, and then spend time making artwork based on these themes. We will explore printmaking, painting, mandalas, masks, and other fun projects. We will also learn how to discuss art and art-making techniques. We will also go on a field trip to see some actual works from different cultures in person. Learn how to be a global citizen, and have fun making art and meeting friends while doing it.

name: English 12: Creat/writ
description: This class is designed to provide workshop experiences and activities which will enhance the communication of ideas through written expression. Students will make choices of their subjects and genres, with interspersed prompts and mini lessons in the craft of writing.

name: AP Physics 1
description: Previous physics is NOT required. This course is equivalent to the first physics class required in college for non-science majors. It fulfills pre-med requirements at many schools and helps prepare students for the MCAT. Students taking this course should have successfully completed or be concurrently enrolled in Secondary Math III. It is a practical course dealing with the principles of motion, forces, energy, momentum, rotation, oscillations, and gravitation. Emphasis is placed on problem solving using a rigorous mathematical treatment; therefore, students must have a solid background in math. Students have the option to take the Calculus-based AP Physics C: Mechanics test instead for those interested in science or engineering careers. Applied Science/Advanced Courses

name: English 12: Film Lit
description: This is an English course, and follows the core curriculum. The specific focus of the class is on the structure and form of visual text (or film). Students will watch, discuss, evaluate, and analyze several short and full-length films with an emphasis on understanding how film functions as a form of literature. Students will learn literary evaluation techniques, critical reading skills, and critical analysis techniques including the use of text-appropriate vocabulary and several theories. Additionally, students will write literary analysis and evaluation essays, concepts, treatments, screenplays, and shooting scripts.

name: Secondary Math III
description: Prior Course Progression Secondary Math II Grades IN SECONDARY MATHEMATICS III students pull together and apply the accumulation of learning that they have from their previous courses, with content grouped into four critical areas, organized into units. They apply methods from probability and statistics to draw inferences and conclusions from data. Students expand their repertoire of functions to include polynomial, rational, and radical functions. They expand their study of right triangle trigonometry to include general triangles. And, finally, students bring together all of their experience with functions and geometry to create models and solve contextual problems. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. All three options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math III Extended Secondary Math III Extended covers the same algebraic and mathematical reasoning concepts as Secondary Math III Regular, but dives deeper into function analysis, trig identities, parametric equations, and polar coordinates. It combines Secondary Math III with precalculus content into a single course and is designed for students planning to take AP Calculus (AB or BC) and AP Statistics in their junior and/or senior years. Secondary Math III Regular Secondary Math III will expand students' understanding of algebraic and mathematical reasoning. The course covers polynomial, rational, and exponential functions, trigonometry, statistics, and an introduction to pre-calculus concepts. Students develop skills in modeling real-world situations and interpreting data. Secondary Math III prepares students for college-level mathematics offered at Timpanogos High School, including AP Statistics, College Algebra (concurrent enrollment), and AP Calculus (AB).

name: Secondary Math II
description: Prior Course Progression Secondary Math 1 THE FOCUS OF SECONDARY MATHEMATICS II is on quadratic expressions, equations, and functions and on comparing their characteristics and behavior to those of linear and exponential relationships from Secondary Mathematics I as organized into six critical areas, or units. The need for extending the set of rational numbers arises, and real and complex numbers are introduced so that all quadratic equations can be solved. The link between probability and data is explored through conditional probability and counting methods, including their use in making and evaluating decisions. The study of similarity leads to an understanding of right triangle trigonometry and connects to quadratics through Pythagorean relationships. Circles, with their quadratic algebraic representations, round out the course. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. Both options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math II Extended Secondary Math II Regular

name: AP Stats
description: AP Statistics is an Advanced Placement course where students will learn how to collect, organize, summarize, and analyze data to gain insights into patterns and trends and to make predictions of future behavior. Probability, counting theory, simulations and mathematical modeling will be studied. Technology will be used to create simulations and analyze data. Full Year Advantage: A full year to learn deeply — compared to a single semester in college. PHYSICAL EDUCATION / HEALTH

The need for extending the set of rational numbers arises, and real and complex numbers are introduced so that all quadratic equations can be solved. The link between probability and data is explored through conditional probability and counting methods, including their use in making and evaluating decisions. The study of similarity leads to an understanding of right triangle trigonometry and connects to quadratics through Pythagorean relationships. Circles, with their quadratic algebraic representations, round out the course. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. Both options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math II Extended Secondary Math II Regular

name: Secondary Math 1
description: Prior Course Progression Intermediate Math 2 THE FUNDAMENTAL PURPOSE OF SECONDARY MATHEMATICS I is to formalize and extend the mathematics that students learned in the middle grades. The critical areas, organized into units, deepen and extend understanding of linear relationships, in part by contrasting them with exponential phenomena, and in part by applying linear models to data that exhibit a linear trend. Secondary Mathematics I uses properties and theorems involving congruent figures to deepen and extend understanding of geometric knowledge from prior grades. The final unit in the course ties together the algebraic and geometric ideas studied. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations.

name: Secondary Math III
description: Prior Course Progression Secondary Math II Grades IN SECONDARY MATHEMATICS III students pull together and apply the accumulation of learning that they have from their previous courses, with content grouped into four critical areas, organized into units. They apply methods from probability and statistics to draw inferences and conclusions from data. Students expand their repertoire of functions to include polynomial, rational, and radical functions. They expand their study of right triangle trigonometry to include general triangles. And, finally, students bring together all of their experience with functions and geometry to create models and solve contextual problems. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. All three options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math III Extended Secondary Math III Extended covers the same algebraic and mathematical reasoning concepts as Secondary Math III Regular, but dives deeper into function analysis, trig identities, parametric equations, and polar coordinates. It combines Secondary Math III with precalculus content into a single course and is designed for students planning to take AP Calculus (AB or BC) and AP Statistics in their junior and/or senior years. Secondary Math III Regular Secondary Math III will expand students' understanding of algebraic and mathematical reasoning. The course covers polynomial, rational, and exponential functions, trigonometry, statistics, and an introduction to pre-calculus concepts. Students develop skills in modeling real-world situations and interpreting data. Secondary Math III prepares students for college-level mathematics offered at Timpanogos High School, including AP Statistics, College Algebra (concurrent enrollment), and AP Calculus (AB).

name: AP Calculus Bc
description: AP Calculus BC covers the topics generally found in a full year college curriculum calculus course. In addition to the topics of limits, derivatives and their applications, and integrals and their applications, students will study the convergence of series, and the calculus of vectors, parametric and polar curves. A heavy emphasis is placed on preparing students to take the advanced placement test in mathematics. A graphing calculator is required for this course.

name: AP Calculus Ab
description: AP Calculus AB covers the topics generally found in a first semester college calculus course. Topics include limits, derivatives and their applications, integrals and their applications, as well as the calculus of trigonometric, logarithmic, and exponential functions. A heavy emphasis is placed on preparing students to take the advanced placement test in mathematics. A graphing calculator is required for this course.

name: AP Calculus Ab
description: AP Calculus AB covers the topics generally found in a first semester college calculus course. Topics include limits, derivatives and their applications, integrals and their applications, as well as the calculus of trigonometric, logarithmic, and exponential functions. A heavy emphasis is placed on preparing students to take the advanced placement test in mathematics. A graphing calculator is required for this course.

name: Lab Calculus
description: Must be enrolled in either Calculus AB or Calculus BC Students taking Advanced Placement Calculus (either AB Calculus or BC Calculus) may enroll in this calculus lab/help class. The purpose of the class is to allow calculus students access to help from their calculus instructor on assignments and prepare for quizzes and tests. Daily activities will include group instruction and activities as well as individualized work. This lab/help class is not required for Advanced Placement Calculus, but can greatly improve the student’s experience and success in their AP course.

name: AP Biology
description: *UVU credit may be earned (Biology 1610/1615 - 5 credit hours, college biology for majors) Recommendations: Good study and attendance habits are necessary prior to enrolling in this course. This course is taught at a college level for students who are interested in continuing their formal education after high school. The class will focus on four big ideas in Biology: 1. The process of evolution drives the diversity and unity of life, 2. Biological systems utilize free energy and molecular building blocks to grow, to reproduce and to maintain dynamic homeostasis., 3. Living systems store, retrieve, transmit and respond to information essential to life processes., 4. Biological systems interact, and these systems and their interactions possess complex properties. Students prepare to take the AP Biology exam. Concurrent enrollment through UVU is available. Science Core Chemistry

name: Computer Science Principles
description: *UVU credit may be earned Computer Science Principles is an introductory computer science course that seeks to broaden participation in computing and computer science. Since the course places emphasis on the principles of computer science rather than just programming, students should expect only to program about 50% of the time, with the rest of the time focusing on understanding big ideas and concepts in the field of Computer Science.

name: Math Decision Making
description: MATH DECISION MAKING is designed to help students who are not currently planning to study Math, Science, and Engineering in college. This course should also be considered by students who will benefit from a review of the core concepts they learned in Secondary Math II and additional instruction for how to use math in more applied settings. The course goal is to use critical thinking to apply students' math skills and abilities in real-life scenarios they will encounter now and after graduation. Students will use statistics, logic, probability, mathematical modeling, and financial management concepts to make decisions, create estimates and forecasts, and set goals for their lives to accomplish the course goals. These concepts include investing for future financial needs, buying insurance, evaluating probabilities, and making reasoned judgments about the world around them. The class covers an entire school year; however, the four quarters of instruction are independent of each other, allowing students to enter and exit the course quarterly as needed. DATA SCIENCE (A/AME) Course Length: Full year This course will develop skills in Data Science and statistics by emphasizing a modeling approach. Students will reason and make sense of data using the Statistical Problem Solving Process, consisting of: 1) Formulating Statistical Investigative Questions, 2) Collect/Consider Data, 3) Analyze Data, and 4) Interpreting the Results. Students will learn to use data and modeling to formulate and answer questions as well as to critically evaluate information using mathematics as the reasoning tool. The class will use the R computer coding language and Jupyter notebooks - two tools used by actual data scientists in the real world. This course is an equivalent replacement for Secondary Math 3. Students could take AP Statistics or Math 1010 after this course.

The need for extending the set of rational numbers arises, and real and complex numbers are introduced so that all quadratic equations can be solved. The link between probability and data is explored through conditional probability and counting methods, including their use in making and evaluating decisions. The study of similarity leads to an understanding of right triangle trigonometry and connects to quadratics through Pythagorean relationships. Circles, with their quadratic algebraic representations, round out the course. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. Both options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math II Extended Secondary Math II Regular

name: Secondary Math III
description: Prior Course Progression Secondary Math II Grades IN SECONDARY MATHEMATICS III students pull together and apply the accumulation of learning that they have from their previous courses, with content grouped into four critical areas, organized into units. They apply methods from probability and statistics to draw inferences and conclusions from data. Students expand their repertoire of functions to include polynomial, rational, and radical functions. They expand their study of right triangle trigonometry to include general triangles. And, finally, students bring together all of their experience with functions and geometry to create models and solve contextual problems. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. All three options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math III Extended Secondary Math III Extended covers the same algebraic and mathematical reasoning concepts as Secondary Math III Regular, but dives deeper into function analysis, trig identities, parametric equations, and polar coordinates. It combines Secondary Math III with precalculus content into a single course and is designed for students planning to take AP Calculus (AB or BC) and AP Statistics in their junior and/or senior years. Secondary Math III Regular Secondary Math III will expand students' understanding of algebraic and mathematical reasoning. The course covers polynomial, rational, and exponential functions, trigonometry, statistics, and an introduction to pre-calculus concepts. Students develop skills in modeling real-world situations and interpreting data. Secondary Math III prepares students for college-level mathematics offered at Timpanogos High School, including AP Statistics, College Algebra (concurrent enrollment), and AP Calculus (AB).

name: Math Decision Making
description: MATH DECISION MAKING is designed to help students who are not currently planning to study Math, Science, and Engineering in college. This course should also be considered by students who will benefit from a review of the core concepts they learned in Secondary Math II and additional instruction for how to use math in more applied settings. The course goal is to use critical thinking to apply students' math skills and abilities in real-life scenarios they will encounter now and after graduation. Students will use statistics, logic, probability, mathematical modeling, and financial management concepts to make decisions, create estimates and forecasts, and set goals for their lives to accomplish the course goals. These concepts include investing for future financial needs, buying insurance, evaluating probabilities, and making reasoned judgments about the world around them. The class covers an entire school year; however, the four quarters of instruction are independent of each other, allowing students to enter and exit the course quarterly as needed. DATA SCIENCE (A/AME) Course Length: Full year This course will develop skills in Data Science and statistics by emphasizing a modeling approach. Students will reason and make sense of data using the Statistical Problem Solving Process, consisting of: 1) Formulating Statistical Investigative Questions, 2) Collect/Consider Data, 3) Analyze Data, and 4) Interpreting the Results. Students will learn to use data and modeling to formulate and answer questions as well as to critically evaluate information using mathematics as the reasoning tool. The class will use the R computer coding language and Jupyter notebooks - two tools used by actual data scientists in the real world. This course is an equivalent replacement for Secondary Math 3. Students could take AP Statistics or Math 1010 after this course.

name: AP Physics 1
description: Previous physics is NOT required. This course is equivalent to the first physics class required in college for non-science majors. It fulfills pre-med requirements at many schools and helps prepare students for the MCAT. Students taking this course should have successfully completed or be concurrently enrolled in Secondary Math III. It is a practical course dealing with the principles of motion, forces, energy, momentum, rotation, oscillations, and gravitation. Emphasis is placed on problem solving using a rigorous mathematical treatment; therefore, students must have a solid background in math. Students have the option to take the Calculus-based AP Physics C: Mechanics test instead for those interested in science or engineering careers. Applied Science/Advanced Courses

name: Natural Resources
description: An instructional course that explores conservation and management of natural resources. The course includes instruction in natural resource science, ecology, population and habitat management, soil and water systems, air quality, rangeland management, waste management, and outdoor recreation safety. Students study local, state, and federal regulations governing natural resource use; evaluate ethical and sustainability issues; analyze population and environmental data; and explore career pathways in natural resource science. SOCIAL STUDIES

name: Aquaculture 1
description: An instructional course that describes the scientific study of the husbandry of fish populations for recreational, ecological, and commercial purposes, and the application of such studies to the management of marine life resources and fisheries. The course includes instruction in principles of aquatic and marine biology, water resources, fishing production and management operations, fishing regulations, water quality monitoring, and the management of recreational and commercial fishing activities.

name: Meteorology
description: Concurrent Enrollment Credit: METO 1010 (UVU) This is a Concurrent Enrollment Course, offering both high school credit through Timpanogos High School and college credit through Utah Valley University. Credit from this course is transferable to all colleges and universities. Introduces the study of our atmosphere. Studies the Earth's dynamic weather systems. Covers structure and compositions of the atmosphere; weather patterns; air masses; and types of weather fronts, weather forecasting, and climates.

name: AP Biology
description: *UVU credit may be earned (Biology 1610/1615 - 5 credit hours, college biology for majors) Recommendations: Good study and attendance habits are necessary prior to enrolling in this course. This course is taught at a college level for students who are interested in continuing their formal education after high school. The class will focus on four big ideas in Biology: 1. The process of evolution drives the diversity and unity of life, 2. Biological systems utilize free energy and molecular building blocks to grow, to reproduce and to maintain dynamic homeostasis., 3. Living systems store, retrieve, transmit and respond to information essential to life processes., 4. Biological systems interact, and these systems and their interactions possess complex properties. Students prepare to take the AP Biology exam. Concurrent enrollment through UVU is available. Science Core Chemistry

name: Biotechnology
description: *UVU credit may be earned (Biotech 1010 - 3 credit hours) This is a laboratory-based course that will introduce students to exciting career opportunities in the field of biotechnology. Students will learn about biotechnology applications in science, industry, medicine and forensics. Students will learn to safely work with bacteria, DNA and proteins. Skills learned in this class will have direct application for students wanting to pursue a career in any biotechnology related fields including medicine. Students enrolled in the course will be eligible for concurrent enrollment at UVU. This CE course fulfills the general life science requirement for graduation at UVU.

name: Zoology
description: This class will survey the biodiversity of animals and their evolutionary relationships. Emphasis will be placed on natural history and comparative anatomy. The class will emphasize science communication and the nature of science as important themes and research projects will include both written and oral presentations. Course requirements will also include mandatory dissections and a species list which will require students to spend time outside observing animals.

name: Biology
description: Students will learn about the structure and function of cells, the patterns, processes, and the environments of living organisms needed, in order to support necessary life functions. They will explore the cause-and-effect relationships of heredity, the role of DNA in gene expression and protein synthesis, and how the mechanisms of genetic variation can lead to diversity within and among species, as well as the great diversity of species as a result of evolution by natural selection. Hands-on, student-centered activities allow students to explore the relevance of biology in their lives.

name: AP Psychology
description: This course is taught at a college level pace for students interested in continuing their formal education after high school. It is for the academically talented student desiring to earn college credit in psychology in the Advanced Placement Program. The purpose of the AP course in psychology is to introduce students to the systematic and scientific study of the behavior and mental processes of human beings and other animals. Students are exposed to the psychological facts, principles, and phenomena associated with each of the major subfields within psychology. They also learn about the methods psychologists use in their science and practice. In addition to studying psychology, students will learn and practice many of the skills that will prepare them to be successful college students.

name: The Holocaust/genocide Studies
description: Holocaust education requires a comprehensive study of not only times, dates, and places, but also the motivation and ideology that allowed these events. In this course, students will study the history of anti-Semitism; the rise of the Nazi party; and the Holocaust, from its beginnings through liberation and the aftermath of the tragedy. The study of the Holocaust is a multi-disciplinary one, integrating world history, geography, and civics. Through this in-depth, semester-long study of the Holocaust, high school students will gain an understanding of the ramifications of prejudice and indifference, the potential for government- supported terror, and they will get glimpses of kindness and humanity in the worst of times.

It emphasizes the manner in which the individual can apply various psychological theories and concepts to better understand oneself, ones motives, and ones relationship with other people.

name: US History
description: This course emphasizes the history of the United States, through the study of basic geographic, economic, social, political, and historical developments following reconstruction and the westward movement. Particular emphasis is placed on the Constitution, the American system of government, law related/citizenship education. Students will identify ways to solve problems, make decisions, and participate as a responsible citizen of the United States, while developing basic tools of historical interpretation, research, and analysis.

name: Sociology
description: This course introduces the student to the principles of sociology. Sociology is the study of people! Students will study society with the goal of learning to critically think like a sociologist and learn to better embrace, accept, and respect themselves and others’ differences. Topics and ideas covered include culture, socialization, social inequalities, crime and deviance, and social work in the real world.

name: Government & Citizenship
description: First or Second Semester This course fosters informed, responsible participation in public life, which is essential to the preservation and improvement of democracy within the United States. Students will be taught the major ideas, protections, privileges, structures, and economic systems that affect the life of a citizen in the United States political system.

name: AP European History
description: AP European History is a rigorous academic course that provides a basic narrative of events and movements in European History from 1450 to the present. It is designed to prepare students for the demands of a college education by providing experience in college level reading and writing, as well as assist you in the preparation for the AP European History Test. AP European is a good class to prepare sophomores to take AP US History or as an upperclassman to enhance what was learned in US History.

name: Sports History
description: Sports History concentrates largely on the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. In addition to examining the history of different sports, particularly those thought of as “national” sports, the course will also explore the Olympics, the rise of the athlete as a popular cultural hero, issues of gender and race in the sporting world, the impact of sports on social mobility, and the extent to which debates within the U.S. sporting world have changed over time.

name: Sports History
description: Sports History concentrates largely on the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. In addition to examining the history of different sports, particularly those thought of as “national” sports, the course will also explore the Olympics, the rise of the athlete as a popular cultural hero, issues of gender and race in the sporting world, the impact of sports on social mobility, and the extent to which debates within the U.S. sporting world have changed over time.

name: AP US History
description: AP US History is a college-level course designed to provide students with a factual knowledge necessary to deal critically with problems and materials in United States history. Along with learning historical content, the course will also train students to analyze and interpret primary sources, including documentary materials, maps, statistical tables, and pictorial and graphic evidence of historical events. The course moves rapidly so it is recommended that only students who read well and have a strong background in US History register for the class. Social Studies Elective

name: AP Government & Politics
description: This course is taught at a college pace for students interested in continuing their formal education after high school. This course will provide students with a general understanding of the governmental, political, and legal systems of the United States. Practical learning skills will be combined with the instruction from outside resource persons and varied activities which will enable students to learn about law and the legal systems of this nation.

name: World Religion
description: World Religions is a comparative course designed to help students gain a better understanding of, and an appreciation for, a variety of religious beliefs, traditions, and practices around the world, thereby helping students develop a general sense of religious tolerance and respect. The course will compare and contrast the major religions of the world, including Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, and Judaism. We will also look into a variety of lesser known religious beliefs and practices.

name: Art History 1010
description: This is a UVU Concurrent Enrollment course that will give an Art 1010 credit. In this class, we will learn about cultures around the world based on themes, and then spend time making artwork based on these themes. We will explore printmaking, painting, mandalas, masks, and other fun projects. We will also learn how to discuss art and art-making techniques. We will also go on a field trip to see some actual works from different cultures in person. Learn how to be a global citizen, and have fun making art and meeting friends while doing it.

name: 2D Art
description: This is an entry-level course where students will explore a variety of drawing and painting materials such as colored pencils, acrylic, charcoal, digital painting, etc. Students will work on developing foundational drawing skills and exploring communicating ideas through artwork. No prior experience required for this course. Come and experiment with new art making materials.

name: 2D Art
description: This is an entry-level course where students will explore a variety of drawing and painting materials such as colored pencils, acrylic, charcoal, digital painting, etc. Students will work on developing foundational drawing skills and exploring communicating ideas through artwork. No prior experience required for this course. Come and experiment with new art making materials.

name: AP Art History
description: Fine Art and/or Social Studies Elective credit may be earned. This course covers the history of all parts of the world, from Europe to Africa to Asia to the Americas, through the lens of the artwork each culture left behind. This course promotes understanding and appreciation of all cultures as students learn more about the history, religions, philosophies, and ideas that shape the world we live in today. These differences are viewed through the artwork created whether buildings, sculptures, or paintings. Students learn to recognize, analyze, and appreciate the artistic skill and deeper themes that artists have communicated across time. 250 works are included in the AP Art History image set, so there is a lot of memorizing, research, and writing in this class. These become life skills that prepare students for college and, more importantly, train students to become critical thinkers as well as open-minded citizens of the world. After taking this class, you will never look at the world the same way.

name: AP Art Drawing/2D Design
description: Suggested Prior Coursework: Honors Art is preferable. Students should come in with drawing and art-making skills. This year-long course is designed to prepare a student to submit an AP Studio Art portfolio. Students spend the year creating an inquiry and then investigating this idea in their artwork. Rather than other art classes where the teacher chooses the assignments, each student has the freedom to make the artwork in the medium and style they want. Because this class is focused on student choice, it is expected that students will come in with artistic experience in how to make art (whether drawing, painting, photography, etc.), composition, and coming up with ideas for their own artwork. Time in class is also used to experiment with art-making, discuss professional and student artist work, and work on the portfolio. Coming up with ideas can be challenging, but is also exciting and rewarding. This is the most advanced art class offered at THS. If making art is something you enjoy doing for fun, this class will really push you to find your own artistic style and voice and prepare you for art classes in college.

name: Art Honors
description: This course is meant to be a deeper full-year study into the basics of art such as drawing, composition, as well as developing creativity and each student’s own artistic ideas. Students will use diverse types of media such as pencil, oil, colored pencil, pastel, watercolor, printmaking, and mosaics (the media studied changes every year). The goal for this class is to have each student leave feeling comfortable with their drawing and painting ability and for each student to be able to communicate their ideas in a clear, well-designed way. This class is geared to get sophomores and juniors ready to take AP Art so seniors are not allowed in the class without permission. This course will be taught one semester by Mrs. Forman and one semester by Ms. Davis, so you students get a chance to learn from both of these teachers.

name: Stained Glass 1
description: This is one of the only high school stained glass classes taught in the country! Come learn how to cut glass and solder it into a beautiful art piece. There is a brief exposure to lead but precautions are taken to keep students safe. You will be making 4-5 stained glass pieces including a small window panel and a 3-D glass project. Anybody can do this so do not worry if you have no previous art abilities.

name: Stained Glass 1
description: This is one of the only high school stained glass classes taught in the country! Come learn how to cut glass and solder it into a beautiful art piece. There is a brief exposure to lead but precautions are taken to keep students safe. You will be making 4-5 stained glass pieces including a small window panel and a 3-D glass project. Anybody can do this so do not worry if you have no previous art abilities.

name: 3D Art
description: This is a great course to further sculpture learning! We will do more unusual 3D arts like stained glass, wire animals, wool felting, and polymer clay. It is NOT a hard class and is an entry level course. Students will be introduced to sculptors, both contemporary and from the past, and learn how to critically evaluate their work and the works of others in the 3-D format.

name: Sculpture
description: This is an entry level course that introduces basic methods for making 3-D art. Mediums such as cardboard, tape, plaster and clay will be used in production. Students will be exposed to many different ways to create art. Great class if you do not like painting or drawing but just making “stuff”.

name: Drawing
description: *UVU Credit may be earned This course replaces Art 1020 at Utah Valley University. It introduces fundamental drawing concepts and media like graphite, charcoal, ink, etc. Requires a sketchbook, in-class and homework assignments. Emphasizes mastery of basic drawing principles like composition, value, line, perspective, and gesture drawing. Drawing from life will be part of this course. The textbook used will be “ A Guide to Drawing” by Faber and Mendelowitz. The class is designed to not only teach drawing skills but prepare students for a college art atmosphere.

name: Painting 1
description: This course is for students who already have some understanding of drawing, but want to extend their artistic experiences into painting. This course builds and strengthens foundations of color, composition, and diverse painting techniques, such as watercolor, acrylic, oil, etc. Students learn painting basics like brush control, color mixing and blending along with exposure to different culture’s art methods. This class is available to students at all skill levels, whether beginning in art or already enrolled in AP Art.

name: Art Honors
description: This course is meant to be a deeper full-year study into the basics of art such as drawing, composition, as well as developing creativity and each student’s own artistic ideas. Students will use diverse types of media such as pencil, oil, colored pencil, pastel, watercolor, printmaking, and mosaics (the media studied changes every year). The goal for this class is to have each student leave feeling comfortable with their drawing and painting ability and for each student to be able to communicate their ideas in a clear, well-designed way. This class is geared to get sophomores and juniors ready to take AP Art so seniors are not allowed in the class without permission. This course will be taught one semester by Mrs. Forman and one semester by Ms. Davis, so you students get a chance to learn from both of these teachers.

name: Stained Glass 2
description: We will be continuing learning stained glass techniques including painting glass and working on the school stained glass mural. Students will have the opportunity to make larger pieces and have more freedom to make what they wish in glass. Lead is used briefly in the solder process but precautions are taken to keep students safe. ART-PERFORMING

name: 2D Art
description: This is an entry-level course where students will explore a variety of drawing and painting materials such as colored pencils, acrylic, charcoal, digital painting, etc. Students will work on developing foundational drawing skills and exploring communicating ideas through artwork. No prior experience required for this course. Come and experiment with new art making materials.

name: Advanced Studio Art
description: Do you want a class where you can work on your own stuff and style? This is it! You will set your own goals and strive towards mastery in the area you choose. We will be discussing portfolio building, careers in art and different media like charcoal, oil painting, pastels, etc. but you will get to choose where to focus your efforts. This is an EXCELLENT class to take alongside AP Art because it will give you time and help to work on your projects due in that class. Please do not take this class without a basic understanding of drawing and/or painting. Sophmores allowed only with permission.

name: Art Foundations II
description: This is an entry-level art course that will explore a number of different media and projects. As the title suggests, this class gives students a chance to explore different styles, media, artists, and processes. Students will work in 2-dimensional media such as drawing and painting but also 3-dimensional media such as sculpture and glass.

name: Drawing
description: *UVU Credit may be earned This course replaces Art 1020 at Utah Valley University. It introduces fundamental drawing concepts and media like graphite, charcoal, ink, etc. Requires a sketchbook, in-class and homework assignments. Emphasizes mastery of basic drawing principles like composition, value, line, perspective, and gesture drawing. Drawing from life will be part of this course. The textbook used will be “ A Guide to Drawing” by Faber and Mendelowitz. The class is designed to not only teach drawing skills but prepare students for a college art atmosphere.

name: Painting 2
description: This course furthers your painting skills with a lot more freedom than Painting 1. The emphasis is on painting animals, still lifes, landscapes and portraits with more exploration in acrylics, oils and watercolor. Additional painting media will be introduced like gouache, digital painting, etc.

name: Chamber Choir
description: Audition Required Chamber Choir is a highly selected small mixed choir. They rehearse and perform significant and challenging choral literature from various periods and styles. Students receive instruction in vocal technique, music literacy, and rehearsal etiquette to prepare them for future participation in college/university, community, or church choirs. Show and Chamber is for experienced singers and musicians who are committed to having a significant choral experience. Members of Show and Chamber are also part of A Cappella. Show and Chamber performs regularly throughout the community and attends several competitions. INSTRUMENTAL

name: Aria Acapella Sa
description: Ladies' A Cappella is a women's chorus aimed at strengthening women's voices and providing opportunities for enjoying a vast selection of fantastic women's music! This Choir rehearses and performs significant women's literature from various periods and styles. Students receive instruction in vocal technique, music literacy, and rehearsal etiquette to prepare them for participation in other auditioned choirs at THS and in future college/university, community, or church choirs. Women's chorus is for singers who want to continue their involvement in choir and improve their musical abilities.

name: Chamber Choir
description: Audition Required Chamber Choir is a highly selected small mixed choir. They rehearse and perform significant and challenging choral literature from various periods and styles. Students receive instruction in vocal technique, music literacy, and rehearsal etiquette to prepare them for future participation in college/university, community, or church choirs. Show and Chamber is for experienced singers and musicians who are committed to having a significant choral experience. Members of Show and Chamber are also part of A Cappella. Show and Chamber performs regularly throughout the community and attends several competitions. INSTRUMENTAL

name: English 12: Music As Literature
description: This is an English 12 or Fine Arts elective course, and students may choose which .50 credit they would like to earn at registration. Music Language Arts explores the power of rhetoric combined with music theory in popular culture throughout history—from Catholic composers working surreptitiously in the courts of Anglican kings to the record-breaking artistry of Taylor Swift; from heartbroken cowboys to Vietnam protests—Civil War songs to African American Spirituals. Sung music provides a detailed record of culture, values, and zeitgeist, which we will analyze for its artistic merit, historical significance, and use of persuasive, argumentative, and expressive language. We will study the music elements of rhythm, harmonic progression, meter, and melodic composition while exploring the rhetorical situation, rhetorical devices, figurative language, and rhetorical appeals as the universal vehicles for carrying language across geographic borders, cultures, peoples, and time.

name: AP Music Theory
description: This course is designed for highly motivated students serious about their study of music. Students will be instructed in music theory, history, sight-singing and tonal dictation. The class requires practice outside of class time as well as book study. The items taught are designed to ensure a good result on the AP Music Theory exam given at the end of the year. This is a valuable class for students interested in continuing their study of music beyond high school.

name: Guitar 1
description: Semester This course is an introduction to playing the guitar. Students will learn chording, note-reading, and rhythms. They will play music pieces ranging from country to classical. name: English 12: Music As Literature
description: This is an English 12 or Fine Arts elective course, and students may choose which .50 credit they would like to earn at registration. Music Language Arts explores the power of rhetoric combined with music theory in popular culture throughout history—from Catholic composers working surreptitiously in the courts of Anglican kings to the record-breaking artistry of Taylor Swift; from heartbroken cowboys to Vietnam protests—Civil War songs to African American Spirituals. Sung music provides a detailed record of culture, values, and zeitgeist, which we will analyze for its artistic merit, historical significance, and use of persuasive, argumentative, and expressive language. We will study the music elements of rhythm, harmonic progression, meter, and melodic composition while exploring the rhetorical situation, rhetorical devices, figurative language, and rhetorical appeals as the universal vehicles for carrying language across geographic borders, cultures, peoples, and time.

name: Chamber Choir
description: Audition Required Chamber Choir is a highly selected small mixed choir. They rehearse and perform significant and challenging choral literature from various periods and styles. Students receive instruction in vocal technique, music literacy, and rehearsal etiquette to prepare them for future participation in college/university, community, or church choirs. Show and Chamber is for experienced singers and musicians who are committed to having a significant choral experience. Members of Show and Chamber are also part of A Cappella. Show and Chamber performs regularly throughout the community and attends several competitions. INSTRUMENTAL

name: Adv Wind Symphony
description: Audition Audition on brass or woodwind instrument required Wind Symphony is the premier concert band at THS. Membership is by audition only, according to ability and instrumentation needs. The audition consists of 6 major scales (1 octave), and sight-reading. Students may also participate in a Spring Tour. The Wind Symphony will also perform with the Orchestra at some concerts. This band is reserved for the dedicated musicians at THS who show self-motivation and a high level of musicianship. Students in the Wind Symphony are required to attend before-school rehearsals every B-day at 7:10 am. Private instruction is highly recommended. Attendance at before, or after school sectionals, rehearsals, festivals and performances is required.

name: Math 1010 Intermediate Algebra
description: 1st Semester Concurrent Enrollment: UVU MATH 1010 (Intermediate Algebra) (4 semester hrs UVU credit) This course expands and covers more in depth basic algebra concepts introduced in Beginning Algebra. Topics of study include linear and quadratic equations and inequalities, polynomials and rational expressions, radical and exponential expressions and equations, complex numbers, systems of linear and nonlinear equations, functions, conic sections, and real world applications of algebra. *NOTE: MATH 1010 will not satisfy math course requirements for the Regents’ Scholarship, unless MATH 1050 is taken semester 2.

name: Intro To Drama And Film
description: Fall & Spring Semester This class is designed for students who are new to theatre, or are interested in the details of film. We will explore the basics of acting as well as dramatic elements found in film. Students will participate as performers and audience members, and take part in discussions regarding the films viewed in class. Students will stretch their abilities to communicate ideas, explore creativity, and collaborate with their peers.

name: Drama 1
description: Drama 1 is an introductory course designed to build confidence, creativity, and communication skills. Students will explore the foundations of acting, including improvisation, vocal projection, and character development. Beyond the stage, students will dive into theatre history, tracing the evolution of storytelling from Ancient Greek tragedies to the modern Broadway stage. Whether you’re an aspiring star or just looking to break out of your shell, this course provides a supportive environment to find your voice.

name: Drama 4
description: Audition Required This class is designed as an advanced course for those students who are serious about performance and acting. Students in this class have been selected, in part, for their maturity and commitment-they are a select group of performers who have been given the opportunity to participate in an aggressive performance schedule. They are expected to continue to sharpen their character analysis skills and performance analysis. Students will participate in the Shakespeare Competition and they will compete in the UHSAA Drama competition. Students are expected to maintain a standard of excellence, being a good example for the rest of the department.

name: Stage Craft
description: This course is designed for students who are interested in the technical and design aspects of theater. They will learn about all areas involved behind the stage in the THS drama department: lights, sound, sets, costumes, props and stage management. There will not be extensive homework in this class, but there is a 30-hour outside of class commitment for crew work.

name: Adv Wind Symphony
description: Audition Audition on brass or woodwind instrument required Wind Symphony is the premier concert band at THS. Membership is by audition only, according to ability and instrumentation needs. The audition consists of 6 major scales (1 octave), and sight-reading. Students may also participate in a Spring Tour. The Wind Symphony will also perform with the Orchestra at some concerts. This band is reserved for the dedicated musicians at THS who show self-motivation and a high level of musicianship. Students in the Wind Symphony are required to attend before-school rehearsals every B-day at 7:10 am. Private instruction is highly recommended. Attendance at before, or after school sectionals, rehearsals, festivals and performances is required.

name: Drama 3
description: This intermediate course is designed for students who have mastered the basics of Drama 1 and 2 and are ready to dive deeper into the professional process of creating theatre. Drama 3 serves as a rigorous preparatory bridge for Drama 4, making it an ideal choice for students looking to refine their craft before entering the advanced production level. Students will move beyond simple performance into the mechanics of playwriting, improvisation, and the study of Shakespeare, while continuing to sharpen their acting techniques and historical knowledge. This course emphasizes the collaborative nature of the arts; students will participate as both dedicated performers and engaged audience members, working together to bring various in-class performance opportunities to life. Whether you are building your portfolio for next year or looking to expand your creative range, this class provides the tools to master the stage.

name: Musical Theater
description: Audition Required This class is designed for students who are serious about performance, and specifically about musical theatre. This class has a high focus on how to improve acting skills within musical theatre performance. Students in this class have been selected for both their singing, acting, and dancing abilities, and also for their willingness to commit to the THS drama department. Students are expected to maintain a standard of excellence, being a good example for the rest of the department. Students will explore various acting methods, singing techniques, dance styles, musical theatre history, and more! All of those areas of study will be focused on as they rehearse and perform in a THS Musical Theatre Showcase. Students will participate as performers and audience members all in an effort to help them become more confident, empathetic, understanding and cooperative.

name: Intro To Drama And Film
description: Fall & Spring Semester This class is designed for students who are new to theatre, or are interested in the details of film. We will explore the basics of acting as well as dramatic elements found in film. Students will participate as performers and audience members, and take part in discussions regarding the films viewed in class. Students will stretch their abilities to communicate ideas, explore creativity, and collaborate with their peers.

name: Drama 2
description: Ready to take your craft to the next level? Drama 2 builds upon the skills established in the introductory course, shifting focus toward technical mastery and stylistic versatility. Students will engage in intensive scene study, exploring various acting methodologies and complex script analysis. We will expand our historical knowledge by examining influential movements such as Elizabethan theatre, Realism, and Avant-Garde performance. This course emphasizes collaborative ensemble work and prepares students for more demanding roles and sophisticated production techniques.

name: Jazz Band
description: Audition Required: Concurrent Enrollment in Symphonic Band, Wind Symphony, Percussion Ensemble, or Orchestra expected This is an advanced instrumental ensemble emphasizing the performance of “big band,” jazz, Latin, swing, and rock styles of music. Jazz improvisation is taught and expected. Auditions will be held at the end of the school year prior to enrollment. Auditions consist of 6 major scales and sight-reading. The band will perform in after school concerts and before school commons’ performances. The Jazz Band performs at Region and possibly State festivals in the spring. The Jazz Band will also play at the girls’ and boys’ basketball games in January and February. Private instruction is highly recommended. Instrumentation for Jazz Band is as follows: Trumpet, Trombone, Alto Sax, Tenor Sax, Baritone Sax, Piano, Bass Guitar, Guitar, Drum set, Vibraphones/Aux. Percussion. Attendance at after school rehearsal, festivals and evening performances is required. ORCHESTRA – INTERMEDIATE A8011/2 (A) No audition is required for participation in this orchestra. Enrollment is open to any student wishing to participate in orchestra. However, students enrolling should have at least a year and preferably two years of experience on their instrument in order to be able to master the literature this orchestra will play. This orchestra will play literature of all genres, typically at Grade Levels 1.5 to 2.5. The music should challenge students, but be within reach of mastery – which is the objective of the Ensemble. Fundamentals of technique and musicianship will be emphasized. Individual practice outside of the Orchestra class is required and though not required, individual private instruction is encouraged. This orchestra typically performs once each term and may participate in a Region Orchestra Festival. A commitment to participate in every performance is required, as is participation at rehearsals both before and after school to in preparation for performances. ORCHESTRA – ADVANCED A8021/2 (A) This is an audition orchestra, with registration by instructor permission only. Individuals who played with this orchestra in a previous year at THS are typically approved to register in subsequent years without further audition. This Orchestra is reserved for students playing at a medium-advanced to an advanced level, who wish to belong to an ensemble willing to tackle difficult music of multiple genres and perform at a high level of excellence. Members are self-motivated and committed to consistent individual practice outside of class rehearsal time. While not required, private instruction is recommended. The Orchestra typically performs once each term, but also performs at Region and potentially State Orchestra festivals and at the annual THS commencement exercises. A commitment to participate in every performance is required. Students also participate in the THS Instrumental Solo & Ensemble Festival, with an opportunity to qualify for both Region and State Festivals. This orchestra may go on a Musicianship tour during the year – with participation by members of the ensemble expected. Members of this ensemble have the opportunity to perform regularly each year with the winds and percussion as a full symphony orchestra and also typically perform with the THS Choirs at a special concert featuring exceptional soloists, as well as for commencement. Attendance at rehearsals and performances both before and after school will be required frequently.

name: Symphonic Band
description: Any band class at high school or junior high during previous academic years or director approval This is the intermediate, non-audition concert band at THS. The class will focus on developing fundamentals in both individual and group performances. Students will perform in multiple concerts and festivals throughout the year. These percussionists may also participate in a Spring Tour. Private instruction is highly recommended. Attendance at some after-school rehearsals, festivals and evening performances is required.

name: Marching Band
description: Fall Concurrent enrollment in a band class at your junior high or high school The Timpanogos Marching Band is a competitive marching band. The band will perform at parades each summer. In the fall, they will participate in marching band competitions. The marching band will also perform at some football games. There is a band camp (July 28-August 8) during the summer, and the band rehearses after school during the school year. Come to the information meeting on May 19th for more information.

name: Intermediate/advanced Percussion
description: Audition Required Audition for Mr. Smith (may be part of taking Beginning percussion) or prior experience–see Mr. Smith In this class, we learn percussion at a more advanced level. We will play mallets, snare drum, timpani, auxiliary percussion, drum set, and much more. Each student is required to learn and become fluent in playing all percussion instruments. This group will perform in multiple concerts. Students will also perform at the Utah Percussive Arts Society Day of the Percussion Festival in the spring. Members of this class will be the percussionists for the Wind Symphony, Orchestra, and most Symphonic Band performances. As members of the Wind Symphony, they will be required to attend before-school rehearsals every B-day at 7:10 am. These percussionists may also participate in a Spring Tour. Private instruction is highly recommended. Attendance at before, or after school sectionals, rehearsals, festivals and performances is required.

name: LA Dance 1
description: Dance is a universal language, an expressive and vibrant art with the capacity to unify the physical, mental, social, emotional, aesthetic, and spiritual. This is a beginning level course which builds dance knowledge, vocabulary, skills in technique, and basic body control. Students will be able to define, demonstrate, and identify the elements that go into all dance styles. Emphasis will be placed on contemporary dance; other units include hip-hop, jazz, ballet, and folk dances. Students will also study criticism and aesthetics.

name: Accounting 1
description: Students will develop skills beginning with an understanding of the basic elements and concepts, double entry accounting systems. Skills include understanding the accounting cycle; entering transactions in journals; posting to ledgers; compiling end of period worksheets; adjusting and closing entries, statements, and reports; completing banking activities and payroll systems. Students will do real life accounting simulations. The state competency test for this area will be given to students who are enrolled in this class. Passing the state test will allow students to show future employers they have a marketable skill.. If you plan on majoring in Accounting or Business Management it is strongly encouraged to take Accounting I in the fall of Junior year and Accounting 2 in the fall of your Senior year.

name: Retailing
description: Retailing is a class that will prepare the student to operate businesses that sell, rent, or lease goods and services. This course will provide activity-oriented training in buying, storing, pricing, advertising, displaying, financing, and other activities necessary for successful business operations. This class runs Titan’s Den (School store during lunchtime and breakfast) which will give you the opportunity to gain retail experience and provide a valuable service to the school community.

name: Business Office Specialist
description: *UVU Credit may be earned This course presents basic computer concepts and hands-on training in using a PC operating system, web browsers, email software, and office application suite software. Students will be exposed to basic terminology of computer hardware and software. Topics covered include: basic computer operating system software commands for efficient computer utilization, use of Internet browsers to locate information for professional and personal use, e-mail basics and netiquette, commands needed to create, edit, enhance, save, print, and effectively use word processing documents, spreadsheets, databases, and presentation slide shows. The state competency test for this area will be given to students who are enrolled in this class. Passing the state test will allow students to show future employers they have a marketable skill.

name: Financial Literacy
description: *UVU Credit may be earned UVU Personal Finance 1060 credit The General Financial Literacy Core is designed for junior and senior students and represents those standards of learning that are essential and necessary for all students. The Utah General Literacy course includes income, money management, spending and credit, saving and investing, consumer protection and risk management. The implementation of the ideas, concepts, knowledge and skills contained in this course will enable students to implement those decision-making skills they must apply and use to become wise and knowledgeable consumers, savers, investors, users of credit, money managers, citizens, and members of a global workforce and society. This course will incorporate concepts and skills from mathematics, language arts, social studies, applied technology, character education, and applied service learning.

description: The focus of this course is for students to gain an understanding of the skills, aptitudes, and thought processes necessary to create a successful marketing campaign using the principles of influencer and content marketing. Through analyzing content, targeting a specific audience in social media, learning user-friendly design and video applications, creating and implementing effective content strategies, and examining the relationship between brands and influencer marketing. Students taking Influencer and Content Marketing should be able to participate in DECA or FBLA career and technical student organizations. These CTSO curriculum and activities are USBE approved for all Utah marketing courses Family and Consumer Science Education

description: Students will understand the aspects of child growth and development, positive guidance techniques, and child-related issues. Learning activities, observation techniques, and lab experiences in working with young children may be included. This course will strengthen comprehension of concepts and standards outlined in Science, Technology, Engineering and Math (STEM) education. Family, Career and Community Leaders of America (FCCLA) supports student leadership and has competitive events that may be an integral part of the course. name: Financial Literacy
description: *UVU Credit may be earned UVU Personal Finance 1060 credit The General Financial Literacy Core is designed for junior and senior students and represents those standards of learning that are essential and necessary for all students. name: AP Physics 1
description: Previous physics is NOT required. This course is equivalent to the first physics class required in college for non-science majors. It fulfills pre-med requirements at many schools and helps prepare students for the MCAT. Students taking this course should have successfully completed or be concurrently enrolled in Secondary Math III. It is a practical course dealing with the principles of motion, forces, energy, momentum, rotation, oscillations, and gravitation. Emphasis is placed on problem solving using a rigorous mathematical treatment; therefore, students must have a solid background in math. Students have the option to take the Calculus-based AP Physics C: Mechanics test instead for those interested in science or engineering careers. Applied Science/Advanced Courses

name: Digital Photo 1
description: *UVU concurrent enrollment credit, Part 1. Part 2 can follow interrupted. 1.2 trillion photos are taken each year, making the understanding and use of Digital Photography more important than ever! Digital Photography is a beginning CTE course that introduces students to the wonderful world of photography. In this course, students will explore the functions of DSLR and Mirrorless cameras, master compositional techniques to clearly communicate ideas, and utilize Adobe Photoshop to edit and enhance their work. In essence, this course is about communication. Students will utilize these skills to develop a personal voice and aesthetic in their photography work that will help prepare them for jobs in this growing industry. Instruction includes training in specialized camera and equipment operation including studio lighting, Adobe Photoshop software usage, maintenance, copyright, ethics, and legal issues in applications to fine art, commercial, industrial, photography business and personal operations. This course is part of the CTE Commercial Photography Pathway!

description: No fee required This course introduces students to the world of commercial art and advertising design. Through hands-on projects, you will develop strong visual communication skills while creating original designs. You’ll build real-world skills used in careers such as graphic design, illustration, branding and art direction. name: Digital Photo 2
description: Part 2 earned with Part 1: Basic Digital Photo 1 from THS *AP Studio 2-D Art Test portfolio preparation for college credit Required materials: access to a DSLR or Mirrorless camera (available for checkout by first come first serve), SD card, USB drive, computer and internet permission This course is the follow-up course to Digital Photo 1. Utilize the skills and techniques learned in Photo 1, Photo 2 students will participate in personally directed work-based projects to achieve mastery in camera functions, composition and voice, and editing techniques to create a portfolio. Students will be expected to do a large amount of work taking photos outside of class. Students are expected to actively communicate ideas and information to business and consumer audiences via the use of advanced digital and/or film photography. Instruction includes training in career opportunities, workflow, specialized SLR digital camera, EOS lenses, studio lighting, memory devices, Adobe Lightroom and InDesign software operation, copyright, ethics, and legal issues as they apply to fine art, commercial and industrial social networking needs for current and future business This course is part of the CTE Commercial Photography Pathway!

name: Exploring Computer Science
description: Exploring Computer Science is designed to introduce students to the breadth of the field of computer science through an exploration of engaging and accessible topics. Rather than focusing the entire course on learning particular software tools or programming languages, the course is designed to focus the conceptual ideas of computing and help students understand why certain tools or languages might be utilized to solve particular problems. The goal of Exploring Computer Science is to develop in students the computational thinking practices of algorithm development, problem solving and programming within the context of problems that are relevant to the lives of today's students. Students will also be introduced to topics such as interface design, limits of computers and societal and ethical issues.

name: Web Development 1
description: Students will learn the basics of web design and development, which includes the foundations of HTML and CSS. code markup, design concepts and web graphics manipulation, page layout, form development, and usability and accessibility issues. Teaches use of Web authoring tools for code development and site management. SKILLED & TECHNICAL SCIENCES EDUCATION (6 areas) Communication:

description: An instructional course that describes the scientific study of the husbandry of fish populations for recreational, ecological, and commercial purposes, and the application of such studies to the management of marine life resources and fisheries. The course includes instruction in principles of aquatic and marine biology, water resources, fishing production and management operations, fishing regulations, water quality monitoring, and the management of recreational and commercial fishing activities.

name: Digital Media 1&2
description: *UVU credit may be earned Semester 1 is designed to provide students with the basic knowledge and skills related to the graphic design industry. It is intended to serve as a starting point for several pathways including Digital Media, Graphics and Printing, 3D Graphics & Animation, and Game Development. Course includes instruction and hands-on assignments in creative design & layout, typography and color using Adobe Photoshop and Illustrator, and teaches computer and professional skills. This yearlong course fulfills both CTE and ART graduation requirements. Semester 2 is designed to give you an in-depth introduction to digital media tools and production techniques. This semester will familiarize you with basic digital media concepts and terms. Programs such as Adobe Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, After Effects, Premiere, Animate and Audition are taught to give you hands-on experience.

name: Digital Business Applications
description: Fall and Spring The business world is progressively more reliant on digital technologies. This course will prepare students with the skills to be an asset to the collaborative, global, and innovative business world of today and tomorrow. Concepts include overall digital experience, digital communications, digital media, presentation skills, social media, career exploration, workplace etiquette and job interviews, and creation of a personalized digital portfolio. Students will also be exposed to cloud computing and Google apps, work with digital images, videos, and audio podcasts, plan a business trip, and collaborate with team members using online tools to create, share and edit files. The goal of this class is to help students gain the knowledge and skills that will enable them to be more marketable by high school graduation.

name: Gaming Development Fundamentals 1/2
description: *UVU credit may be earned Using Unity and C# scripting or Unreal Engine, this course is designed to provide students with knowledge and project based experience of fundamental gaming development concepts relating to STEM. These concepts include game design, scripting, creation of digital assets, graphic resources, animations, understanding hardware, problem solving, critical thinking, collaboration, and project management. Students will be expected to complete a write-up of the student’s own design of a game in a Game Design Document (GDD) by the end of the semester. In the second semester, students will be working in a team to develop their own game that they have designed in the previous semester. This course has a concurrent enrollment (CE) option when taken as a full year course.
