name: Computer Programming, Advanced
description: *UVU credit may be earned *UVU credit may be earned This course builds on the concepts introduced in Computer Programming 1 and 2 and introduces students to introductory data structures, classes and objects, recursive functions, searching and sorting algorithms, and other more advanced topics. Students may earn Concurrent Enrollment (CE) credit from UVU. Note: the course will be taught in C++

name: AP Computer Science A
description: Year *UVU credit may be earned AP Computer Science A emphasizes object-oriented programming methodology with an emphasis on problem solving and algorithm development and is meant to be the equivalent of a first semester undergraduate course in computer science. It also includes the study of data structures and abstraction. Note: the course will be taught in Java. Students registering for this class can opt to have a Concurrent Enrollment (CE) option to obtain UVU credit instead of AP.

name: Linux Fundamentals
description: Year *UVU credit may be earned The successful junior level Linux administrator should be able to: Work at the Linux command line; Perform easy maintenance tasks including assisting users, adding users to a larger system, executing backup and restore, shutdown and reboot; Install and configure a workstation (including X) and connect it to a LAN, or a stand-alone PC via modem to the Internet. Students will be well on their way to be ready to pass industry certification (CompTIA Linux+) by the end of the course.

name: Principles Of Cyber Defense
description: *UVU credit may be earned In this course, students will explore the current cyber threat landscape to understand the types of adversaries organizations face and the techniques adversaries use to compromise systems and data, how vulnerabilities create risks, and how organizations implement security controls to manage those risks. Students will research emerging trends in cybersecurity and gain hands-on experience implementing security protocols.

name: Computer Programming 2
description: *UVU credit may be earned *UVU credit may be earned This course introduces students to more complex data structures and their uses, including sequential files, lists, graphical user interface (GUI),, and classes. Students will learn to create more powerful programs within a specific programming language. Both Computer Programming 1 and 2 are taken together for Concurrent Enrollment (CE) credit from UVU. Note: the course will be taught in Python.

name: ASL 2
description: Builds on experiences gained in ASL 1. Course is taught with immersion and a comprehensible approach to language learning. Emphasizes stronger expressive and receptive conversational skills through active student participation. Includes more information on Deaf culture, Deaf history, Deaf issues, and ASL history.

name: Intro To Health Science
description: Semester The Introduction to Health Occupations course is a fun, fast-paced class for 10th or 11th grade students interested in a possible career in the health and medical field. Students will be involved in activity-centered lessons which explore medical careers, utilize technology, and teach body systems. Students will perform several dissections, learn to take blood pressure, and be introduced to medical terminology. This class will prepare students to take Medical Anatomy/Physiology, Sports Medicine, Biotechnology, Advanced Health Science and the other health science courses offered at THS.

name: ASL 3
description: Students will learn advanced conversational and performance skills in this course. your fluency level in this course will increase and you will be able to converse in most real-life situations. You will continue to learn about Deaf culture and will cover a variety of topics in more depth. Evaluations will be based on performance, receptive skills, written evaluations and papers.

description: Year Intro to IT CE recommended *UVU credit may be earned Students will learn necessary competencies for an entry-level IT professional including installing, building, upgrading, repairing, configuring, troubleshooting, optimizing, diagnosing, and performing preventive maintenance of basic personal computer hardware. name: Internship
description: Application Required. Must be on a CTE Pathway. This course will offer opportunities for students to learn in the workplace with a mentor. Students who apply for this program will be placed with sponsoring community businesses or agencies as unpaid interns. Students must have related CTE classes and/or be on a CTE Pathway to participate. Required hours at the internship is a minimum of 40 per semester, per class period in the internship class. Classroom seminars and a written portfolio are part of this program. Students apply in the spring and must be accepted into the program. Applications are available on the school website, in counseling and from the internship coordinator. CTE Internships are found in every CTE Pathway.

name: Fitness
description: PE: $10 Participation Fee (+) $10 Uniform Fee This course is an individualized, concepts-based, one-semester course designed to give students the knowledge and skills necessary to self-assess, create, conduct, evaluate, and redesign personal fitness programs. This course is a combination of classroom and activity based learning activities that focus on proper nutrition and fitness concepts necessary for students to monitor and improve their personal lifetime fitness. Through participation in individual and team activities and personal fitness assessments, students will learn the benefits of living a healthy lifestyle. This class fills the Fitness for Life course required for graduation and there are no substitutions allowed for this credit, including participation in athletics.

name: Yoga
description: Fall or Spring Semester Yoga 101 is designed to introduce students to the fundamentals of yoga practice, including various poses, breathing techniques, and mindfulness exercises. This course aims to improve flexibility, strength, and mental well-being while providing students with tools to manage stress and promote relaxation. Course curriculum will focus on learning foundational poses and vinyasas, proper alignment, and basic breathing and meditation practices. Instruction will cater to different fitness levels and abilities, allowing modifications for individual needs.

name: Sports History
description: Sports History concentrates largely on the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. In addition to examining the history of different sports, particularly those thought of as “national” sports, the course will also explore the Olympics, the rise of the athlete as a popular cultural hero, issues of gender and race in the sporting world, the impact of sports on social mobility, and the extent to which debates within the U.S. sporting world have changed over time.

name: Wgt Train
description: This class is a one semester course and has been designed to introduce students to the basic principles and safety techniques utilized in weight training. The overall purpose of this class is to demonstrate, through class participation, how the use of weight training can be effective in helping to develop a healthy lifestyle. The curriculum meets the needs of individual students and emphasizes physical fitness and proper nutrition. This class fills elective credit needed for graduation.

The overall purpose of this class is to demonstrate, through class participation, how the use of weight training can be effective in helping to develop a healthy lifestyle. The curriculum meets the needs of individual students and emphasizes physical fitness and proper nutrition. This class fills elective credit needed for graduation.

name: Fitness
description: PE: $10 Participation Fee (+) $10 Uniform Fee This course is an individualized, concepts-based, one-semester course designed to give students the knowledge and skills necessary to self-assess, create, conduct, evaluate, and redesign personal fitness programs. This course is a combination of classroom and activity based learning activities that focus on proper nutrition and fitness concepts necessary for students to monitor and improve their personal lifetime fitness. Through participation in individual and team activities and personal fitness assessments, students will learn the benefits of living a healthy lifestyle. This class fills the Fitness for Life course required for graduation and there are no substitutions allowed for this credit, including participation in athletics.

name: Sports History
description: Sports History concentrates largely on the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. In addition to examining the history of different sports, particularly those thought of as “national” sports, the course will also explore the Olympics, the rise of the athlete as a popular cultural hero, issues of gender and race in the sporting world, the impact of sports on social mobility, and the extent to which debates within the U.S. sporting world have changed over time.

name: Emergency Medical Responder (emr)-introduction Ems
description: Semester This class is recommended for Grades 11-12, with acceptance of motivated 10th grade students. This semester course provides students with the knowledge and skills, including Professional CPR/AED certification, that will prepare them to act in emergency medical situations. Students will have hands-on practice of emergency rescuer skills and guest speakers from different medical science careers associated with emergency medicine. The student can qualify for the American Red Cross Emergency Medical Responder Certification card upon successful completion of all requirements.

name: Intro To Health Science
description: Semester The Introduction to Health Occupations course is a fun, fast-paced class for 10th or 11th grade students interested in a possible career in the health and medical field. Students will be involved in activity-centered lessons which explore medical careers, utilize technology, and teach body systems. Students will perform several dissections, learn to take blood pressure, and be introduced to medical terminology. This class will prepare students to take Medical Anatomy/Physiology, Sports Medicine, Biotechnology, Advanced Health Science and the other health science courses offered at THS.

name: Health
description: Fall or Spring Semester This class is designed to help students progress toward becoming a Healthy, Happy, Competent Citizen. Health topics covered are mental health, substance abuse prevention, nutrition, healthy relationships, and other related areas.

name: Medical Terminology
description: *UVU CE Credit may be earned This is a one-semester course that is intended for students who are interested in pursuing a career in health care. It is also for students who desire to heighten their ability to communicate and understand medical jargon. This course stresses terminology usage and accuracy. We will study the elements, abbreviations, spelling, pronunciation, and logic of medical terminology. Some understanding of general anatomy will be taught as well. Instruction includes lectures, audiovisual presentations, and hands-on activities. INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY EDUCATION

name: Peer Tutor PE
description: This is a one semester course designed to offer opportunities to participate in a wide variety of physical activities with students in the Life Skills class. The curriculum emphasizes teamwork and team building rather than competitiveness. Some of the activities taught in this class are: basketball, volleyball, curing, snowshoeing, weight lifting and yoga. These activities coincide with Unified PE tournaments throughout the year. The curriculum provides diverse activities to meet the individual needs of all students and develops competency in several sports and lifetime activities.

name: Life Activ 1
description: This is a one semester course designed to offer instruction in several different individual and group lifetime activities. The curriculum emphasizes individual and dual sports and the need for developing quality fitness habits throughout life. Activities taught in this class are: racquetball, handball, indoor soccer, ultimate frisbee, bouldering, bowling, tennis, softball, golf, table tennis, land paddling, volleyball, and softball. The curriculum provides diverse activities to meet the individual needs of all students and develops competency in several sports and lifetime activities.

name: Secondary Math II
description: Prior Course Progression Secondary Math 1 THE FOCUS OF SECONDARY MATHEMATICS II is on quadratic expressions, equations, and functions and on comparing their characteristics and behavior to those of linear and exponential relationships from Secondary Mathematics I as organized into six critical areas, or units. The need for extending the set of rational numbers arises, and real and complex numbers are introduced so that all quadratic equations can be solved. The link between probability and data is explored through conditional probability and counting methods, including their use in making and evaluating decisions. The study of similarity leads to an understanding of right triangle trigonometry and connects to quadratics through Pythagorean relationships. Circles, with their quadratic algebraic representations, round out the course. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. Both options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math II Extended Secondary Math II Regular

name: Secondary Math III
description: Prior Course Progression Secondary Math II Grades IN SECONDARY MATHEMATICS III students pull together and apply the accumulation of learning that they have from their previous courses, with content grouped into four critical areas, organized into units. They apply methods from probability and statistics to draw inferences and conclusions from data. Students expand their repertoire of functions to include polynomial, rational, and radical functions. They expand their study of right triangle trigonometry to include general triangles. And, finally, students bring together all of their experience with functions and geometry to create models and solve contextual problems. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. All three options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math III Extended Secondary Math III Extended covers the same algebraic and mathematical reasoning concepts as Secondary Math III Regular, but dives deeper into function analysis, trig identities, parametric equations, and polar coordinates. It combines Secondary Math III with precalculus content into a single course and is designed for students planning to take AP Calculus (AB or BC) and AP Statistics in their junior and/or senior years. Secondary Math III Regular Secondary Math III will expand students' understanding of algebraic and mathematical reasoning. The course covers polynomial, rational, and exponential functions, trigonometry, statistics, and an introduction to pre-calculus concepts. Students develop skills in modeling real-world situations and interpreting data. Secondary Math III prepares students for college-level mathematics offered at Timpanogos High School, including AP Statistics, College Algebra (concurrent enrollment), and AP Calculus (AB).

name: Secondary Math 1
description: Prior Course Progression Intermediate Math 2 THE FUNDAMENTAL PURPOSE OF SECONDARY MATHEMATICS I is to formalize and extend the mathematics that students learned in the middle grades. The critical areas, organized into units, deepen and extend understanding of linear relationships, in part by contrasting them with exponential phenomena, and in part by applying linear models to data that exhibit a linear trend. Secondary Mathematics I uses properties and theorems involving congruent figures to deepen and extend understanding of geometric knowledge from prior grades. The final unit in the course ties together the algebraic and geometric ideas studied. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations.

Emphasis will be on improving proficiency in all language skill areas with special emphasis dedicated to improving reading and writing skills. Students should remember that this course leads to earning college credits, therefore, it will be run as such. Students are expected to be responsible for their learning and are expected to attend class on a regular basis. Class instruction will be given in Spanish. Students have the option of taking the AP Spanish Language Exam in May.

name: Spanish 1
description: $5 Fee required Students begin to communicate sufficiently to satisfy basic survival needs and minimum courtesy requirements. They can ask and answer some simple questions, respond to and sometimes initiate short statements and maintain simple conversation. Students understand and interpret written and spoken language on a variety of topics. Students will begin to identify and react to perspectives and practices of the Spanish culture.

name: Spanish 2
description: $5 Fee required Students develop further communication skills and are able to satisfy most survival needs and limited social demands. They can initiate and sustain a short conversation on factual topics. They can give autobiographical information and discuss leisure time activities. Students are able to present information and ideas to an audience. They can describe characteristics and behaviors of everyday life in the Spanish culture and they can identify the expressive forms of the culture, such as art, literature, music and dance.

name: Student Council
description: Fees required for summer workshop and uniform. This class provides elected officers the opportunity to learn how to operate student school governments.

name: Pack
description: Application Required Fees required for summer workshop and uniform. This course focuses on service and on the qualities of leadership. Students work to develop these qualities in the lives of students and participate in and lead service projects and activities. The PACK also teams with Windsor Elementary School in Kid’s Cause to meet the needs of Windsor’s families.

name: Sources Of Strength
description: Teacher Approval Fees required for summer workshop and uniform. $50+ Sources of Strength is a leadership coalition at Timpanogos High School that works to change unhealthy norms and culture, ultimately preventing suicide, bullying, and substance abuse. The mission of Sources of Strength is to prevent unhealthy activities by increasing help seeking behaviors and promoting connections between peers and caring adults. Sources of Strength teaches students to cultivate positive aspects of a person's life like family support, positive friends, mentors, healthy activities, generosity, spirituality, physical health, and mental health. Sources of Strength involves peer leadership and positive messaging campaigns to spread awareness within our school community.

name: Latinos In Action (lia - Peer Leaders) Uslssl
normalizedName: latinos in action (lia - peer leaders) uslssl
description: UVU/Concurrent Enrollment Credit possible Teacher Approval/Application This is a class and club that teach the principles of leadership and the importance of service through service projects and teamwork. Students also learn the power they can have as a bilingual as they are trained in reading strategies that will help them be effective tutors and mentors to Latino children at neighboring Elementary and Jr. High schools. The first 2 months of the course are spent in training and then the bulk of the following months are spent tutoring in the schools.

name: Ths Content Creation
description: Applicaiton Required Fees required Content Creation allows students to gain real-world experience creating website articles, social media content, and print media for Timpanogos High School. Students will use the THS website and social media channels to disperse information to our students, faculty, and community. Some after-school work may be required.

name: Medical Anatomy Physiology
description: *UVU credit may be earned via the Ed. Net class only This full year course provides students with an in depth study of health care careers, including actual clinical experience in therapeutic, diagnostic, information, and environmental health care settings. Instruction includes intermediate anatomy and physiology, medical terminology, diseases and disorders, medical ethics, and first aid. The class is designed to prepare students for the advanced health science course and/or for a variety of health technology programs.

name: Medical Terminology
description: *UVU CE Credit may be earned This is a one-semester course that is intended for students who are interested in pursuing a career in health care. It is also for students who desire to heighten their ability to communicate and understand medical jargon. This course stresses terminology usage and accuracy. We will study the elements, abbreviations, spelling, pronunciation, and logic of medical terminology. Some understanding of general anatomy will be taught as well. Instruction includes lectures, audiovisual presentations, and hands-on activities. INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY EDUCATION

name: Secondary Math III Extended
description: Secondary Math III Extended covers the same algebraic and mathematical reasoning concepts as Secondary Math III Regular, but dives deeper into function analysis, trig identities, parametric equations, and polar coordinates.
